title: The Zuckerberg First Meeting: What Silence Reveals About Founders

source.original: Marc Andreessen: Will a16z Go Public & Why Labour Displacement with AI is Wrong (20VC)

body_html:
Andreessen's most memorable first founder meeting was with 19-year-old Mark Zuckerberg. The setup: Zuckerberg and Sean Parker arrive together. Sean talks nonstop — "every idea, absolutely amazing, a mile a minute." Zuckerberg doesn't say a single word.

Andreessen's real-time assessment: "One of two things has happened here. Either he's completely unsuited for the job because he literally doesn't talk. Or he's listening and absorbing everything people say around him, and he's going to be on a vertical learning curve like crazy because he doesn't have the ego need to just say things."

It was the latter. This anecdote encapsulates Andreessen's broader framework for evaluating founders. The loudest person in the room isn't necessarily the most capable. The person who listens may be processing information at a rate that will translate into rapid adaptation. Zuckerberg's silence wasn't weakness — it was absorption. He didn't need to perform competence through talking; he demonstrated it through learning.

This cuts against the stereotypical founder archetype — the charismatic visionary who commands a room with rhetoric. Andreessen explicitly notes that "some of the best founders in history were not very likable people." The same is true of great artists, filmmakers, political leaders. The qualities that make someone effective at building aren't the qualities that make them pleasant to be around. If you're selecting founders based on how much you enjoy their company, you're optimizing for the wrong variable.
